annotations.text: Dysarthria, nasal speech, diplopia, and vertigo all suggest brain-stem dysfunction.
The most common disease involving both the large and small vessels is atherosclerosis, and for that reason, anticoagulation therapy was begun.
The pattern matches the description by Wilkinson and Russell in their classic review of giant-cell arteritis.
Although this elderly, hypertensive man probably did have some atheromatous disease, the vessels involved and the laboratory evidence suggest that he had giant-cell arteritis.
The vessels in giant-cell arteritis are usually not involved after they perforate the dura; at this location, they lose elastic fibers in the media, and the external elastic lamina disappears.
Given this anatomical structure, Wilkinson and Russell hypothesized that the underlying pathological process is probably the result of an autoimmune reaction involving at least the elastic tissue.

A rate of 47 mm per hour or more is a finding that is 92 percent sensitive for giant-cell arteritis, and an elevated level of C-reactive protein (a markedly elevated level in this case, at 29.8 mg per liter) is 100 percent sensitive.
If the erythrocyte sedimentation rate is considered along with the C-reactive protein level, the specificity for giant-cell arteritis is 97 percent.
The possibility of an antiphospholipid-antibody syndrome deserves consideration in this case.
Another disease that should be considered in a discussion of the differential diagnosis of cerebral vasculitis is intravascular lymphomatosis, a non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.

The presence of pulmonary nodules should also raise the possibility of lymphomatoid granulomatosis, an angiocentric and angiodestructive lymphoproliferative disease usually regarded as yet another unusual form of lymphoma.
The histopathological features, the distribution of the affected arteries, and the absence of antibodies specific to myeloperoxidase or proteinase 3 rule out an ANCA-related vasculitis.
Current thinking is that they represent an epiphenomenon rather than a phenomenon intrinsic to the pathogenesis of giant-cell arteritis.
The process seems to begin with the accumulation of interferon-secreting CD4 T cells and macrophages expressing tumor necrosis factor in the adventitia of elastic arteries.
In contrast, the actual injury to the internal elastic lamina is mediated by non–interferon-secreting CD4 T cells and collagenase-secreting macrophages.
